result:
The first thing that comes is the sound of wind in leaves, a hushing that makes every childhood tree house feel like a cathedral. I am nine again, barefoot on rough bark, convinced that if I press my cheek to the trunk long enough the oak will tell me its real name. It never does, but the sparrows offer gossip in exchange for crumbs, and I learn that language is not only what we speak aloud.

At fifteen I discover the word saudade, Portuguese for a longing so large it has its own gravity, and I realize I’ve been orbiting that dark star since before I could spell it. Every song on the cheap transistor radio is a map back to a home that never existed. I tape the charts off Sunday-night countdowns, worship in the low-fi hiss, and believe that if I can just memorize the lyrics to “Fast Car” the future will unlock like a permission slip. Years later, when Tracy Chapman’s voice comes on in a grocery aisle, I still feel the plastic-coated wire of the headphones, the scratch of synthetic carpet in the basement where I practiced being anyone but myself.

Memory is not a library; it’s a lagoon. You row in circles, convinced you’re exploring, while the same drowned bikes glint beneath you. The mind rearranges the furniture every night, so yesterday’s prison becomes tomorrow’s safe house. I have been, in no particular order: altar boy, shoplifter, ghostwriter of love letters for boys too shy to speak, biology major who fainted at the first incision, overnight cashier who read Kierkegaard between beer runs, husband who learned to fold fitted sheets like origami cranes, father who can recite “Goodnight Moon” in the dark without skipping the line about the quiet old lady whispering hush.

There is a secret interstate below the interstate: storm drains wide enough for two kids on BMX bikes, graffiti that changes dialect every fifty feet, the smell of wet concrete and something metallic, like the air itself is rusting. We raced down there one summer, chasing the echo of our own laughter, certain we’d emerge in Narnia or at least the next county. Instead we found a shopping cart with a missing wheel and a sofa soaked to twice its weight. On the fabric, someone had written in Sharpie: “We were here before the water.” I think about that line whenever the news shows another flood. We are all before the water, until we aren’t.

I once believed adulthood would feel like mastery instead of perpetual improvisation. No one mentioned the fine print: how freedom and loneliness share a duplex, how you can stand in the cereal aisle and feel the full vertigo of choice—suddenly responsible for every consequence of oats. My friend Mara says we’re all just kids wearing the skins of our parents, trying to remember when to pay the power bill. She keeps a list of heroic failures: the man who spent twenty years carving a tunnel to his lover’s house only to emerge in the neighbor’s pantry; the woman who trained crows to bring her coins and wound up funding a small library. “Failure is just success perpendicular,” she insists, pouring wine into a coffee mug that reads World’s Okayest Intern.

The body is a liar that insists on honesty. It remembers what the mind negotiates away: the burn on my palm from a sparkler in 1992, the exact temperature of a hospital waiting room in 2008. I used to think death lived outside the fence, a prowler we could keep out with vitamins and seatbelts. Turns out it’s a tenant, subletting the spare room, borrowing our dishes, leaving notes on the fridge: Milk expires, why not we? The first time I saw a dead person he looked embarrassed, like he’d shown up to Halloween without a costume. The second time, she looked infinitely relieved, as if she’d finally recalled the name of that tune she’d been humming for seven decades.

There are whole years I spent waiting for permission: to write, to leave, to say I love you without sounding like I was asking a question. Meanwhile the planet spun like a dog shaking off water, shedding species and coastlines. One Tuesday I quit my job because the sky was the precise color of a postcard from 1954 and I wanted to be useful before the shade vanished forever. I learned to repair bicycles, to translate between Spanish and grief, to plant milkweed like tiny flags of surrender. Monarchs folded themselves into my palms, drunk on nectar, wings the color of burnt toast and stained glass. I released them and felt lighter, as if I’d handed off a classified message I wasn’t cleared to read.

Love arrives disguised as trespass. A person sits in your kitchen, buttering toast without asking, and two years later her toothbrush is a monument you walk past in the dark. We speak in shorthand about the mundane: Did you let the cat in, where’s the good can opener, did you hear that sound like the furnace giving up? One night I watched her sleep, the way her eyelids fluttered like moths caught in a lampshade, and understood that devotion is not thunder but the steady drip that carves a canyon through limestone. I wanted to apologize to every rock I ever dismissed for being too slow.

Some mornings I wake with the taste of iron in my mouth, relic of a dream where I’m building a bridge out of license plates. Each state arrives dented, sun-bleached, carrying the weight of every mile it never intended to go. I weld them together with sparks that smell like burnt toast, aware the river below is rising but unable to recall which songs the crew sang in the old cartoons to make work feel like play. A beaver shows up wearing a hard hat, offers me a blueprint drawn on birch bark. In the margin it says: Begin in the middle.

I keep a notebook of last sentences. Not for novels—though I’ve written three no one wants—but for exits: leaving a party, a job, a body. The best so far is “I forgot to water the gratitude plant,” spoken aloud to a room already empty. Second best: “The map was accurate, but the world changed.” I practice them the way some people keep go-bags by the door. Readiness is its own superstition.

Yet here I am, still inside the moment before the last sentence, palming minutes like marbles I haven’t decided to keep or lose. Outside, the streetlights flicker on because the sky has decided to forgive us again. Somewhere a child is discovering the difference between alone and lonely. Somewhere else an old man is buttoning his good shirt for the final time, fingers like moths at the lamp of each pearl disc. I want to tell them both: pay attention to the hush between engines, the way the dark smells of bread and thunder, the fact that every breath is a letter you haven’t yet addressed.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
